text.title: IEntityTypeConfiguration Pattern
text.summary: EF Core entity configuration using IEntityTypeConfiguration for clean, maintainable data-layer setup.

rule.statement: Extract every entity's configuration into a separate IEntityTypeConfiguration<T> class. Never inline builder calls in OnModelCreating beyond ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly.
rule.rationale: OnModelCreating becomes unmaintainable past 5 entities when configurations are inlined. Separate files allow focused review and independent version history per entity.

rule.statement: In OnModelCreating, call only modelBuilder.ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly(typeof(AppDbContext).Assembly). No entity-specific calls.
rule.rationale: Single-call convention makes the context trivially testable and allows new configurations to be discovered automatically without editing the context class.

rule.statement: Use HasConversion for value objects (Money, Email). Use OwnsOne for owned entities. Use HasDiscriminator only when polymorphic queries are needed.
rule.rationale: HasConversion keeps the domain model free of EF attributes. Owned types map cleanly to columns without a join. Discriminator inheritance adds query complexity that is rarely needed.

rule.statement: Index every foreign key explicitly. EF Core does not add FK indexes automatically in some provider/version combinations.
rule.rationale: Missing FK indexes cause full table scans on joins; the performance regression only appears under production data volumes.

antipattern.description: Using OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade) on multiple paths in SQL Server without checking the migration SQL.
antipattern.reason: SQL Server rejects FK cycles caused by multiple cascade paths, producing a migration error only at apply time, not at code review time.

antipattern.description: Omitting RowVersion or ConcurrencyCheck on aggregate roots.
antipattern.reason: Without optimistic concurrency, last-write-wins silently corrupts data under concurrent updates.

text.reference: Full IEntityTypeConfiguration skeleton with common patterns.
